title: Designing a Preference Evaluation Set
concept: Build diverse prompt test sets matching real user query distributions, and use clear scoring rubrics for human raters. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: LLM-as-a-Judge.'

exercises.prompt: What are the two key ingredients needed to design a great preference evaluation set?

options.text: A diverse set of prompts and a clear scoring rubric.
A random list of words with no grading rules.

exercises.explain: To evaluate a model fairly, we need a balanced set of test prompts and a standardized way (a rubric) for humans to score the outputs.

cards.title: Testing the Limits
cards.body: How do you know if your AI chatbot is actually getting better? You can't just guess! You need a systematic, fair way to test it. Let's learn how to build a perfect test suite that reflects real-world users and keeps human graders on the same page.

cards.title: What is an Evaluation Set?
cards.body: A Preference Evaluation Set is a curated collection of diverse prompts used to test an AI. It mimics what real users will ask. To measure the AI's success fairly, human raters grade the AI's answers using a strict, step-by-step scoring rubric.

cards.title: The Restaurant Tasting Panel
cards.body: Imagine you are opening a new restaurant. If you only test your menu on your brother, you won't know if the general public likes it. You need a tasting panel of different people—some vegetarians, some spice lovers, and some kids—to taste and rate your dishes using a clear scorecard. Just like that, testing an AI requires a diverse 'tasting panel' of prompts and a clear scoring sheet.

cards.title: Putting It Into Practice
cards.body: If your AI is a homework helper, your evaluation set shouldn't just have math questions. It should include science questions, grammar corrections, and even silly questions. Human raters then grade the answers from 1 to 5 using a rubric that defines exactly what a '5' answer looks like (accurate, polite, and clear).

cards.title: More Prompts Aren't Always Better
cards.body: Many think that to test an AI, you just need a list of 1,000 random prompts. In reality, quantity doesn't beat quality. A small, diverse, and well-balanced set of 100 prompts that represents real user habits is much more valuable than 1,000 repetitive or random prompts.

cards.title: Comparing Test Methods
cards.body: See how a structured evaluation process compares to an unstructured, chaotic one.

right.text: Testing with diverse, realistic prompts and grading with a strict, shared rubric.

left.text: Testing the AI with random prompts and grading its answers based on 'gut feeling'.

exercises.prompt: How should you choose the prompts for your evaluation set?

options.text: They should match the actual distribution of real user queries.
They should all be extremely easy so the AI always passes.
They should focus only on coding questions.
They should be randomly copied from dictionary definitions.

exercises.explain: To know if an AI is ready for the real world, the test questions must match the variety and style of questions real users will actually ask.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Without a clear scoring rubric, human raters might grade the same AI answer differently.
exercises.explain: Without a clear rubric, different human graders will apply their own bias and standards, making the test results inconsistent and unreliable.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about AI evaluation.
exercises.sentence: To ensure human raters grade AI answers consistently, they must follow a detailed scoring [blank].
exercises.explain: A rubric provides clear guidelines to human raters so that everyone grades the AI's answers using the same standards.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following responses is the most helpful explanation of an evaluation set?

options.text: Text A: "I don't know anything about evaluating AI models. Ask someone else."
Text B: "An evaluation set is a curated collection of diverse prompts tested against a clear grading rubric."
Text C: "To evaluate an AI, you just ask it one question and see if it looks correct."

exercises.explain: Text B is the best response because it directly and clearly answers the user's question, whereas Text A and C either fail to answer or give incorrect advice.

exercises.prompt: You want to launch a new chatbot assistant. Which approach will give you the most accurate and reliable feedback on its performance?

options.text: Test it only with extremely difficult, long math equations to push its limits.
Create a diverse set of prompts reflecting real-world use cases, evaluated against a clear rubric.
Ask a single coworker to chat with the AI for five minutes and see if they like the vibe.
Only test prompts that the AI has already seen during its training process.

exercises.explain: A great evaluation set mirrors real usage, covers edge cases, and uses a strict rubric to keep ratings consistent. Next up: LLM-as-a-Judge.
